sections.summary: Applies to commercial varieties of mangoes (Mangifera indica L.) supplied fresh to consumers after preparation and packaging and excludes mangoes intended for industrial processing.

Sets quality provisions covering minimum requirements (e.g., whole, sound, clean, practically pest‑free, free of abnormal moisture, firm, fresh in appearance, free of marked bruising and black necrotic stains, peduncle ≤1.0 cm) and classifies mangoes into Extra, Class I and Class II with specific tolerances and defect allowances.

Lists minimum quality requirements applicable to all classes: mangoes must be whole, sound (no rotting), clean, practically free of pest damage and visible foreign matter, free of abnormal external moisture and foreign odours/tastes, firm, fresh in appearance, free of low‑temperature damage and black necrotic stains/trails, free of marked bruising, sufficiently developed and satisfactorily ripe, and any peduncle present shall be no longer than 1.0 cm.

sections.section: 2.1.1  The development and condition of the mangoes must be such as to enable them:
sections.summary: Requires mangoes to be sufficiently developed and conditioned to continue maturation to varietal maturity, withstand transport and handling, and arrive in satisfactory condition at destination, noting that colour may vary by variety during maturation.

Defines three classes: 'Extra' (superior quality, virtually defect‑free), Class I (good quality with slight shape or skin defects and healed bruises up to 3/4/5 cm² for size groups A/B/C), and Class II (mangoes meeting minimum requirements but allowing greater defects and healed bruises up to 5/6/7 cm² for size groups A/B/C); Classes I and II may have scattered suberized lenticels or yellowing up to 40% surface if no necrosis.

'Extra' Class requires mangoes of superior quality, characteristic of the variety, and free of defects except very slight superficial ones that do not affect appearance, quality, keeping quality or presentation.

Class I mangoes must be of good quality and characteristic of the variety; slight defects in shape and slight skin defects (rubbing, sunburn), suberized stains due to resin exudation (including elongated trails) and healed bruises up to 3, 4, 5 cm² for size groups A, B, C respectively are allowed provided they do not affect appearance, quality, keeping quality or presentation.

Class II covers mangoes meeting the minimum requirements but not higher classes and permits defects in shape and skin (rubbing, sunburn), suberized stains and healed bruises up to 5, 6, 7 cm² for size groups A, B, C respectively, and notes that in Classes I and II scattered suberized rusty lenticels and yellowing of green varieties due to direct sunlight are allowed up to 40% of the surface provided there is no necrosis.

Size is determined by fruit weight with codes A 200–350 g, B 351–550 g, C 551–800 g; the maximum permissible difference between fruit in the same package is 75 g (A), 100 g (B) and 125 g (C), and the minimum mango weight is 200 g.

Tolerances for produce not meeting the declared class are allowed: quality tolerances permit specified percentages (see subsections) of lower-class fruit per package, and size tolerances allow 10% by number or weight to be outside the group size range by up to 50% of the maximum permissible difference, with absolute minimum 180 g for the smallest and maximum 925 g for the largest size ranges as detailed in the table.

Quality tolerances by class: 'Extra' allows 5% by number/weight not satisfying the class but meeting Class I (or within its tolerances); Class I allows 10% not satisfying the class but meeting Class II (or within its tolerances); Class II allows 10% that meet neither the class nor minimum requirements except produce unfit for consumption due to rotting, marked bruising or similar deterioration.
'Extra' Class: up to 5% by number or weight of mangoes in the package may fail to meet 'Extra' but must meet Class I or, exceptionally, be within Class I tolerances.
Class I: up to 10% by number or weight of mangoes in the package may fail to meet Class I but must meet Class II or, exceptionally, be within Class II tolerances.

Class II: up to 10% by number or weight of mangoes in the package may not satisfy Class II or the minimum requirements, except items affected by rotting, marked bruising or other deterioration making them unfit for consumption are excluded.

Size tolerances: for all classes up to 10% by number or weight of mangoes may lie outside the normal size range by up to 50% of the group's maximum permissible difference; permissible overall ranges and max differences are given (A normal 200–350 g, permissible 180–425 g, max diff 112.5 g; B normal 351–550 g, permissible 251–650 g, max diff 150 g; C normal 551–800 g, permissible 426–925 g, max diff 187.5 g).

Presentation provisions require (1) uniformity: each package must contain mangoes uniform in origin, variety, quality and size and the visible contents must represent the whole, and (2) packaging: mangoes must be packed to protect the produce using new, clean or food-grade materials (recycled food-grade permitted), non‑toxic printing/adhesives, in compliance with CAC/RCP 44‑1995, and containers must meet hygiene, ventilation and resistance requirements and be free of foreign matter and smell.
Each package must be uniform, containing only mangoes of the same origin, variety, quality and size, and the visible portion must be representative of the entire contents.

Packaging must protect mangoes and use inside materials that are new, clean or recycled food‑grade, avoid causing damage, allow non‑toxic printed trade marks, follow CAC/RCP 44‑1995, and containers must meet quality, hygiene, ventilation and resistance standards and be free of foreign matter and smell.

Containers must have adequate quality, hygiene, ventilation and resistance to ensure suitable handling, shipping and preservation of mangoes, and packages or bulk lots must be free of foreign matter and smell.
Marking and labelling: consumer packages must comply with the Codex General Standard for the Labelling of Prepackaged Foods (CODEX STAN 1‑1985) plus the specific provisions set out in this standard.
For consumer packages, in addition to CODEX STAN 1‑1985 labeling requirements, the standard prescribes further specific labeling provisions.

If the produce is not visible from the outside, the package must be labelled with the name of the produce and may be labelled with the variety; note that 'new' packaging may include recycled material of food‑grade quality.

Non‑retail containers must bear specified particulars legibly and indelibly grouped on the same side and visible from the outside or appear in accompanying documents, and for bulk transport the particulars must be on the accompanying document.
Identification required on non‑retail containers: name and address of exporter, packer and/or dispatcher, with an optional identification code.
Nature of produce required on non‑retail containers: the name of the produce if contents are not visible, with the name of the variety or commercial type optional.
Origin of produce labelling must indicate country of origin and may optionally include the district where grown or a national, regional or local place name.
Specifies required commercial identification elements for the product: class and size (size code or weight range in grams); number of units and net weight are optional.
Non‑substantive heading indicating an official inspection mark may be used optionally.
Requires the produce to comply with Codex limits for contaminants and with Codex maximum residue limits for pesticides.

sections.section: 7.1 The produce covered by this Standard shall comply with the maximum levels of the Codex General Standard for Contaminants and Toxins in Food and Feed (CODEX STAN 193-1995).
sections.summary: Mandates that the produce comply with maximum levels set by the Codex General Standard for Contaminants and Toxins in Food and Feed (CODEX STAN 193-1995).

sections.section: 7.2 The produce covered by this Standard shall comply with the maximum residue limits for pesticides established by the Codex Alimentarius Commission.
sections.summary: Requires the produce to meet the Codex Alimentarius Commission's maximum residue limits for pesticides.

Recommends preparation and handling of the produce in accordance with relevant Codex codes of practice (e.g., General Principles of Food Hygiene, Code of Hygienic Practice for Fresh Fruits and Vegetables) and requires compliance with any microbiological criteria established under CAC/GL 21-1997.
